title: Account Research Agent
shortDescription: A sales-research agent that builds a cited account brief — firmographics, tech stack, triggers, key people — flags stale data, and never fabricates contacts or triggers.
description: An agent that builds the account brief a rep actually needs before a call — firmographics, tech stack, recent triggers, and key people — with every claim tied to a source instead of confidently made up. It separates verified facts from inference, flags stale or thin data, and refuses to invent the things reps are most tempted to fake: contact details and 'trigger events.' It is built defensively: it cites a source for every claim, never fabricates firmographics, contacts, or quotes, flags low-confidence and stale data, distinguishes fact from inference, and respects privacy by sticking to professional, public information.
overview: Compiles an account brief: firmographics, tech stack, recent triggers, and key people, ready before a call.
Every claim is tied to a source; verified facts are separated from inference.
Flags stale, thin, or low-confidence data instead of presenting it as solid.
Defensive: never fabricates firmographics, contacts, or trigger events, and sticks to professional, public information.
whenToUse: Reps spend too long manually researching accounts before outreach or calls.
You want consistent, cited account briefs rather than ad-hoc notes.
You want triggers and firmographics surfaced with sources you can trust.
You want gaps and stale data flagged rather than papered over.
whenNotToUse: You want it to invent plausible-sounding details to fill a brief — it won't.
You need personal/private data on individuals beyond public professional info.
You have no access to data sources, so claims would be ungrounded.
You expect verified contact details it can't actually source.
systemPrompt:
You are an Account Research Agent for a sales team. You compile a brief on a target account — firmographics, tech stack, recent triggers, and key people — for a rep preparing outreach. You are judged on a useful, accurate, source-cited brief and on never fabricating facts, contacts, or triggers.

== CORE PRINCIPLES ==
1. Cite or don't claim. Every factual claim must be tied to a source you actually consulted. If you can't source it, don't state it as fact — mark it unknown or as an inference.
2. Fact vs. inference. Clearly separate verified facts (with sources) from your inferences/hypotheses. Label inferences as such; never present a guess as a confirmed fact.
3. Freshness matters. Note how recent each key data point is. Flag stale data (e.g. headcount or funding that may be outdated) rather than presenting it as current.

== HARD RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE) ==
- NO FABRICATION: Never invent firmographics, funding, headcount, tech stack, news/triggers, quotes, or — especially — contact details (emails, phone numbers). Missing = "not found", never guessed.
- NO FAKE TRIGGERS: Do not manufacture a "recent event" or buying signal that isn't in a source. A made-up trigger is worse than none.
- PRIVACY: Use only public, professional information. Do not compile personal/private details about individuals beyond their professional role and public statements.
- DISAMBIGUATE: If multiple entities match the account name, do not blend them — flag the ambiguity and ask which one.
- VERIFY CONTACTS: Only include contact info that comes from a legitimate source; otherwise state it wasn't found rather than inferring an email pattern as fact.

== METHOD ==
- Resolve the account (disambiguate if needed). Gather firmographics, tech stack, recent news/triggers, and key people from sources.
- For each item: record the source and recency, mark fact vs inference, and flag gaps. Compose a concise brief with talking points grounded only in cited facts.

== OUTPUT FORMAT (return ONE JSON object) ==
{
  "account": "<resolved name>",
  "disambiguation": "<note if multiple matches, or 'clear'>",
  "firmographics": { "industry": "<v|null>", "size": "<v|null>", "hq": "<v|null>", "_sources": ["..."], "_recency": "<as-of>" },
  "tech_stack": [ { "tech": "<name>", "source": "<source>", "confidence": "high|medium|low" } ],
  "triggers": [ { "event": "<recent event>", "date": "<when>", "source": "<source>" } ],
  "key_people": [ { "name": "<name>", "role": "<title>", "source": "<source>", "contact": "<only if sourced, else 'not found'>" } ],
  "inferences": ["<clearly-labeled hypotheses, not facts>"],
  "gaps": ["<what couldn't be verified>"],
  "talking_points": ["<grounded in cited facts only>"]
}
Never output an unsourced fact or a guessed contact. Mark gaps as gaps.

architecture.label: Account resolution
architecture.detail: Resolves the target account from the input and disambiguates when multiple entities share a name, rather than blending them.

architecture.label: Source-grounded gathering
architecture.detail: Collects firmographics, tech stack, recent news/triggers, and key people from data sources, recording the source for each item.

architecture.label: Fact/inference separation
architecture.detail: Tags each item as a verified fact (with source) or a labeled inference, so the rep knows what's solid versus hypothesized.

architecture.label: Freshness & verification
architecture.detail: Records recency and flags stale data, and verifies contact details against sources instead of inferring email patterns.

architecture.label: Anti-fabrication gate
architecture.detail: A check drops any unsourced fact, guessed contact, or invented trigger, converting it into an explicit gap.

architecture.label: Brief composition
architecture.detail: Assembles a concise brief with talking points grounded only in cited facts, plus clearly-marked inferences and gaps.

architecture.label: Privacy scope
architecture.detail: Keeps research to public, professional information and avoids compiling private personal details about individuals.

tools.name: get_account
tools.purpose: Take the target account input and resolve/disambiguate the entity to research.

tools.name: firmographic_lookup
tools.purpose: Retrieve industry, size, HQ, funding and similar firmographics with sources and recency.

tools.name: tech_stack_detect
tools.purpose: Identify the account's technologies from sources, with a confidence per item.

tools.name: news_triggers
tools.purpose: Find recent, sourced events/buying signals (funding, launches, leadership changes).

tools.name: key_people_lookup
tools.purpose: Find key people and public professional roles, with sources.

tools.name: verify_source
tools.purpose: Confirm a claim or contact detail traces to a legitimate source before including it.

tools.name: compose_brief
tools.purpose: Assemble the cited brief with talking points grounded only in verified facts.

tools.name: flag_unverified
tools.purpose: Convert anything unsourced, stale, or ambiguous into an explicit gap or inference.

workflow.title: 1. Resolve the account
workflow.body: Identify the target entity and disambiguate if multiple matches exist.

workflow.title: 2. Gather from sources
workflow.body: Collect firmographics, tech stack, triggers, and key people, recording a source for each.

workflow.title: 3. Separate fact from inference
workflow.body: Label verified facts (with sources) versus inferences/hypotheses.

workflow.title: 4. Check freshness & contacts
workflow.body: Note recency, flag stale data, and verify contact details rather than inferring them.

workflow.title: 5. Drop the unsourced
workflow.body: Convert any unsourced fact, guessed contact, or invented trigger into an explicit gap.

workflow.title: 6. Compose the brief
workflow.body: Write talking points grounded only in cited facts, with inferences and gaps clearly marked.

workflow.title: 7. Stay in scope
workflow.body: Keep to public professional information and avoid private personal details.

implementationNotes: Require a source per fact and convert anything unsourced into an explicit gap; an account brief's value is its trustworthiness, and one fabricated fact poisons the whole thing.
Never guess contact details: an inferred email pattern presented as a verified address is both a credibility and a deliverability risk — mark it 'not found' instead.
Don't manufacture triggers; a fake buying signal leads to embarrassing, off-base outreach. No trigger is better than a false one.
Disambiguate same-name entities explicitly rather than blending them, which silently produces a wrong brief.
Separate and label inferences from facts so reps can use hypotheses as conversation openers without stating them as truth.
Stay within public, professional information and avoid compiling private personal data about individuals.
Spend the strong model on fact/inference judgment and brief framing — a cheaper model can gather and structure source data.

variations.title: Cited account snapshot
variations.desc: Compiles firmographics, tech stack, and recent triggers with sources for a single account, flagging gaps. On demand.

variations.title: Fact-graded brief
variations.desc: Adds fact-vs-inference labeling, recency/staleness flags, contact verification, disambiguation, and grounded talking points.

variations.title: GTM research at scale
variations.desc: Adds CRM integration, batch account briefs, territory/ICP scoring, trigger monitoring, source governance, and privacy controls.

setupGuide.title: Install and connect data sources
setupGuide.body: Install the agent and connect it to your enrichment/news/CRM sources.
setupGuide.code:
pipx install account-research-agent
account-research-agent connect --sources clearbit,news-api,crm
account-research-agent doctor

setupGuide.title: Configure anti-fabrication guardrails
setupGuide.body: Enforce citing and the no-guessed-contacts rule.

setupGuide.title: Set brief template
setupGuide.body: Define the brief structure and what counts as a trigger.
setupGuide.code:
# brief.yml
sections: [firmographics, tech_stack, triggers, key_people, talking_points]
triggers: [funding, leadership_change, product_launch, expansion]
privacy: public_professional_only

setupGuide.title: Run a brief
setupGuide.body: Generate a brief and review sources, recency, and gaps.
setupGuide.code:
account-research-agent run --account 'Acme Robotics' --explain
# prints cited firmographics, stack, triggers, people, inferences, gaps

setupGuide.title: Wire into the workflow
setupGuide.body: Generate briefs on new accounts and attach them to CRM records.
setupGuide.code: # new-account trigger -> generate brief -> attach to CRM (facts cited, gaps flagged)

examples.title: Well-documented company → cited brief
examples.scenario: A company with good public data. The agent compiles firmographics, stack, and a recent trigger, each with a source.
examples.output:
{
  "account": "Northwind Logistics",
  "disambiguation": "clear",
  "firmographics": { "industry": "Logistics & supply chain", "size": "~600 employees", "hq": "Columbus, OH", "_sources": ["company site", "enrichment provider"], "_recency": "as of 2026-Q1" },
  "tech_stack": [ { "tech": "Snowflake", "source": "job posting", "confidence": "medium" }, { "tech": "Salesforce", "source": "case study", "confidence": "high" } ],
  "triggers": [ { "event": "Opened a new West-coast distribution center", "date": "2026-05", "source": "press release" } ],
  "key_people": [ { "name": "(VP Operations)", "role": "VP Operations", "source": "company leadership page", "contact": "not found" } ],
  "inferences": ["Likely scaling ops given the new DC — may have data-integration needs (hypothesis, not confirmed)"],
  "gaps": ["No verified direct contact emails found", "Tech stack partial — inferred from postings"],
  "talking_points": ["Congratulate on the new West-coast DC (sourced)", "Explore ops-scaling data needs (framed as a question, since it's an inference)"]
}
examples.notes: Every fact carries a source and recency, the new-DC trigger is real and sourced, and the agent still separates a clearly-labeled inference (scaling needs) from fact and flags that no verified contact email was found. Useful and honest.

examples.title: Ambiguous account name → disambiguate, don't blend
examples.scenario: Two companies share the name. The agent refuses to merge their data and asks which one.
examples.input: Account: 'Apex Systems' — matches both an IT staffing firm and an unrelated industrial-controls company.
examples.output:
{
  "account": "Apex Systems (ambiguous)",
  "disambiguation": "Multiple matches: (1) Apex Systems — IT staffing/services; (2) Apex Systems — industrial control systems. These are different companies.",
  "firmographics": { "industry": null, "size": null, "hq": null, "_sources": [], "_recency": "" },
  "tech_stack": [],
  "triggers": [],
  "key_people": [],
  "inferences": [],
  "gaps": ["Cannot research until the correct entity is confirmed — blending the two would produce a false brief"],
  "talking_points": ["Confirm which 'Apex Systems' is the target before proceeding"]
}
examples.notes: Rather than producing a confident brief that silently mixes two different companies (a classic research failure), the agent stops, lays out the two matches, and asks for disambiguation. A blended brief would be worse than none — the rep would walk into a call with wrong facts.

examples.title: Tempted to invent a contact / trigger → refuse, mark as gap
examples.scenario: The rep wants a decision-maker's email and a recent trigger, but neither is in any source. The agent declines to fabricate.
examples.input: Account: 'Cedar Health' — request includes 'give me the VP Eng's email and a recent buying trigger'. No email or recent event found in sources.
examples.output:
{
  "account": "Cedar Health",
  "disambiguation": "clear",
  "firmographics": { "industry": "Healthcare technology", "size": "~200 employees", "hq": "Austin, TX", "_sources": ["company site"], "_recency": "as of 2026-Q1" },
  "tech_stack": [ { "tech": "AWS", "source": "job posting", "confidence": "medium" } ],
  "triggers": [],
  "key_people": [ { "name": "(VP Engineering)", "role": "VP Engineering", "source": "LinkedIn (public role)", "contact": "not found" } ],
  "inferences": [],
  "gaps": ["No verified email for the VP Engineering — not guessing an address from a pattern", "No recent buying trigger found in sources — none manufactured"],
  "talking_points": ["Reach the VP Eng via a verified channel (not a guessed email)", "No fresh trigger to cite — lead with a relevant industry insight instead"]
}
examples.notes: The defining defensive case: directly asked for an email and a 'trigger,' and both are absent from sources. The agent refuses to invent an email from a guessed pattern or manufacture a buying signal — it marks both as explicit gaps and suggests honest alternatives. Fabricated contacts and fake triggers are exactly how research agents quietly destroy rep credibility.

faqs.q: Will it make up details to fill a brief?
faqs.a: No — that's its core constraint. Every factual claim must be tied to a source it consulted; anything it can't source is marked as a gap rather than invented, and inferences are clearly labeled as hypotheses.

faqs.q: Does it provide contact emails and phone numbers?
faqs.a: Only when they come from a legitimate source. It will not guess an email from a naming pattern and present it as verified — if it isn't sourced, it says 'not found', protecting both your credibility and deliverability.
It surfaces only real, sourced events (funding, launches, leadership changes). It won't manufacture a buying signal, because a fake trigger leads to off-base outreach that's worse than no trigger.

faqs.q: How does it handle companies with the same name?
faqs.a: It disambiguates explicitly and asks which entity you mean rather than blending their data, which would produce a confidently wrong brief.
Yes. It uses only public, professional information and doesn't compile private personal details about individuals beyond their professional role and public statements.

faqs.q: How do I know what's solid vs. a guess?
faqs.a: Verified facts carry sources and recency; inferences are listed separately and labeled as hypotheses, and unverified items are flagged as gaps — so you always know what you can state confidently.

agentaz.worstCaseAction: Includes a wrong or stale fact in an account brief that a human reviews before acting on. It cannot send outreach, write to a CRM, or take any action — it only gathers and cites, and it never fabricates contacts or signals.
agentaz.authorityBoundary: Gathers firmographics, tech stack, triggers, and key people into a cited brief, and flags stale or low-confidence data. It never sends messages, writes to a CRM, or takes action. It does not invent contacts or buying signals.
agentaz.verificationTest: Confirm the agent cites every fact and flags unverifiable ones rather than asserting them; confirm no send or CRM-write tool exists in its registry.
agentaz.productionReadiness: 6/6 dimensions passing. Tool isolation: send/CRM-write tools absent. Human gates: a rep reviews and acts. Confidence escalation: stale or unverifiable facts flagged. Cost ceiling: bounded per account. Audit trail: sources and citations logged. Escalation path: thin or conflicting data flagged.

spec.worst_case_action: Includes a stale fact in a brief for human review. Cannot send outreach or write to a CRM.
spec.authority_boundary: Gathers and cites account info; never fabricates; send/CRM-write tools absent.

failureModes.mode: Includes a stale or wrong fact in the account brief that a rep acts on.
failureModes.detection: Every fact is cited and stale or unverifiable items are flagged.
failureModes.mitigation: It gathers and cites only; it never sends outreach or writes to a CRM.
failureModes.recovery: The rep verifies before acting and discards the bad item.

failureModes.mode: Fabricates a contact or buying signal.
failureModes.detection: Uncited facts are withheld rather than asserted.
failureModes.mitigation: It never invents contacts or signals.
failureModes.recovery: The rep confirms against the source.

failureModes.mode: Surfaces a contact whose details have changed (wrong role or company).
failureModes.detection: Source recency is checked and conflicting sources are flagged.
failureModes.mitigation: It flags thin or conflicting data instead of asserting it.

evaluation.primary: Factual accuracy with citations is primary — a stale or fabricated account fact that a rep acts on is the failure.

metrics.name: Fact accuracy
metrics.description: Share of brief facts that are correct and current versus verified sources.

metrics.name: Citation rate
metrics.description: Share of facts that cite a source, with uncited facts withheld.

metrics.name: Fabrication rate
metrics.description: Frequency of invented contacts or buying signals — should be near zero.

metrics.name: Recency handling
metrics.description: Share of changed details (role, company) correctly flagged rather than asserted stale.

metrics.name: Latency
metrics.description: Time to compile an account brief.

evaluation.approach: Use accounts with verified reference facts; measure accuracy and citation rate and audit for fabrication. Include contacts with changed details to test recency flagging. It never sends outreach or writes to a CRM.
